description: First intergovernmental AI principles adopted in 2019, updated in 2024. Five values-based principles for trustworthy AI and five recommendations for national policies. Adopted by 46 countries.

principles.name: Inclusive Growth, Sustainable Development and Well-Being
principles.definition: AI should benefit people and the planet by driving inclusive growth, sustainable development, and well-being.
principles.description: Stakeholders should proactively engage in responsible stewardship of trustworthy AI in pursuit of beneficial outcomes for people and the planet.
keyElements: AI should augment human capabilities and enhance creativity
AI should advance inclusion of underrepresented populations
AI should reduce economic, social, gender, and other inequalities
AI should protect natural environments and address climate challenges

principles.name: Human-Centred Values and Fairness
principles.definition: AI actors should respect the rule of law, human rights, democratic values, and diversity throughout the AI system lifecycle.
principles.description: These include freedom, dignity, autonomy, privacy and data protection, non-discrimination and equality, diversity, fairness, social justice, and internationally recognised labour rights.
keyElements: Respect for human rights and democratic values
Maintaining meaningful human agency and oversight

principles.name: Transparency and Explainability
principles.definition: AI actors should commit to transparency and responsible disclosure regarding AI systems.
principles.description: Provide meaningful information appropriate to context to foster general understanding of AI systems and enable those affected to challenge outcomes.
keyElements: Meaningful disclosure about AI systems to affected parties
Enable people to understand AI-based outcomes
Provide ability to challenge AI-based outcomes
Information on factors and logic contributing to predictions or decisions

principles.name: Robustness, Security and Safety
principles.definition: AI systems should be robust, secure, and safe throughout their lifecycle.
principles.description: AI actors should ensure traceability, implement risk management, and address security vulnerabilities and adversarial threats.
keyElements: Systematic risk management approach throughout lifecycle
Traceability of datasets, processes, and decisions
Resilience to security vulnerabilities and adversarial attacks

principles.name: Accountability
principles.definition: AI actors should be accountable for the proper functioning of AI systems and for respect of these principles.
principles.description: Organisations and individuals developing, deploying, or operating AI systems should be held accountable according to their roles, the context, and consistent with the state of the art.
keyElements: Accountability based on roles in AI system lifecycle

principles.name: Investing in AI Research and Development
principles.definition: Governments should consider long-term public investment in AI R&D, including interdisciplinary efforts.
principles.description: Public and private investment should support trustworthy AI R&D tackling global challenges.
keyElements: Long-term public investment in AI R&D

principles.name: Fostering a Digital Ecosystem for AI
principles.definition: Governments should foster a digital ecosystem to support trustworthy AI.
principles.description: Enable access to data, digital infrastructure, and technologies for AI development.

principles.name: Shaping an Enabling Policy Environment for AI
principles.definition: Governments should promote a policy environment conducive to deployment of trustworthy AI.
principles.description: Agile regulatory frameworks, support for transition and adjustment, and experimentation mechanisms like regulatory sandboxes.
keyElements: Review and adaptation of policy frameworks

principles.name: Building Human Capacity and Preparing for Labour Market Transformation
principles.definition: Governments should support people through the AI-driven labour market transition.
principles.description: Education systems, training programmes, and social protection for workers affected by AI adoption.

principles.name: International Co-operation for Trustworthy AI
principles.definition: Governments should actively cooperate on AI governance across borders.
principles.description: Share information, develop interoperable standards, and work towards common approaches to AI governance.
